meta.title: Mathematics Graduate Study Plan
meta.subtitle: NCEA Level 3 → Graduate-level Mathematics
meta.end_goal: Graduate-level mathematical competency with synthesis toward AI, stochastic finance, and numerical computation

phases.label: Pre-Phase
phases.name: Proof Readiness
phases.goal: Build the logical language and proof techniques used in every subsequent module.

phases.label: Phase 1
phases.name: Structural Foundations
phases.goal: Fluency in the three pillars of undergraduate mathematics: multivariable calculus, linear algebra, and computation.

phases.label: Phase 2
phases.name: Mathematical Rigour
phases.goal: Transition from using tools to proving they work. Real Analysis and Abstract Algebra are the intellectual core.

phases.label: Phase 3
phases.name: Advanced Core
phases.goal: Final-year BSc and early graduate content: Complex Analysis, Topology, PDEs, Numerical Analysis.

phases.label: Phase 4
phases.name: Synthesis & Specialisation
phases.goal: Graduate-level integration. Measure Theory as foundation; then branch to Finance (Track A) or ML (Track B).

modules.name: Logic, Sets, and Mathematical Proof
modules.description: The single biggest shift from school maths to university maths is moving from computing answers to proving statements. This module builds the logical language and proof techniques used in every subsequent module. Without it, Phase 2 courses (Real Analysis, Abstract Algebra) will be inaccessible.
modules.why_it_matters: Every theorem you will ever prove rests on the machinery built here. Students who skip this struggle permanently with Analysis and Algebra.
learning_outcomes: Construct and evaluate logical arguments using propositional and predicate logic.
Work fluently with set notation, power sets, Cartesian products, and cardinality.
Write correct direct proofs, proofs by contradiction, and proofs by contrapositive.
Apply mathematical induction (weak and strong) to prove statements about all natural numbers.
Understand and negate quantified statements (∀, ∃) without logical error.
Recognise when a proof is incomplete or incorrect.
modules.milestone: Prove that √2 is irrational. Prove there are infinitely many primes. Write proofs using ε-notation informally as a warm-up for Analysis.
modules.ai_integration: Proof checking: paste a proof draft and ask for logical gaps. Counterexample hunting: ask for concrete examples that break a false conjecture.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.090 Introduction to Mathematical Reasoning

modules.name: Single Variable Calculus
modules.description: NCEA Level 3 covers the mechanics of differentiation and integration. This module extends and formalises that knowledge — L'Hôpital's rule, improper integrals, sequences, series, and Taylor polynomials — and introduces the why behind the techniques.
modules.why_it_matters: The foundation for every analytical tool in the programme. Multivariable Calculus, ODEs, and Real Analysis all build directly on this.
learning_outcomes: Calculate limits rigorously, including indeterminate forms via L'Hôpital's rule.
Apply differentiation to related rates, optimisation, and curve analysis.
Evaluate definite and improper integrals using substitution, integration by parts, and partial fractions.
Determine convergence and divergence of sequences and series using standard tests.
Construct Taylor and Maclaurin polynomials and use them for approximation.
Use polar coordinates and parametric equations.
modules.milestone: Evaluate ∫ x·ln(x) dx from scratch. Determine whether Σ(1/n²) converges and find its value. Write the Taylor series for sin(x) and prove its convergence.
modules.ai_integration: Ask for worked examples at each new technique. Ask to explain geometrically what the series convergence tests are really doing.

modules.name: Linear Algebra
modules.description: Linear algebra is the language of AI, data science, physics, and virtually all quantitative fields. Follows Strang's Four Fundamental Subspaces approach, building toward SVD and eigenanalysis.
modules.why_it_matters: Nearly every advanced module uses matrices, vector spaces, or eigenanalysis. This is the most practically useful single module in the programme.
learning_outcomes: Solve systems of linear equations via Gaussian elimination; compute LU decomposition.
Define and identify vector spaces, subspaces, bases, and dimension.
Find the four fundamental subspaces (column space, null space, row space, left null space) of a matrix.
Apply the Gram-Schmidt process and construct orthogonal projections.
Solve least-squares problems for overdetermined systems.
Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors; diagonalise matrices.
Understand and compute the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
Use linear algebra for geometric transformations, Markov chains, and network analysis.
modules.milestone: Given a matrix A, find all four fundamental subspaces and verify the rank-nullity theorem. Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Compute the SVD of a 2×2 matrix by hand.
modules.ai_integration: Ask for geometric interpretations of each subspace. Ask for ML applications of each concept (SVD → image compression, PCA, recommendation systems).

modules.name: Multivariable Calculus
modules.description: Extends calculus to functions of several variables — the setting for virtually all real-world modelling. Green's, Stokes', and the Divergence Theorems are the climax.
modules.why_it_matters: The gradient is the key tool of machine learning. Surface integrals and vector fields appear throughout physics and finance. This module is the bridge from 1D to high-dimensional mathematics.
learning_outcomes: Calculate partial derivatives, gradients, and directional derivatives.
Apply the multivariable chain rule and implicit differentiation.
Find and classify critical points of multivariable functions; apply Lagrange multipliers.
Evaluate double and triple integrals in rectangular, polar, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates.
Compute line and surface integrals.
State and apply Green's Theorem, Stokes' Theorem, and the Divergence Theorem.
Understand the gradient as the direction of steepest ascent (connection to gradient descent in ML).
modules.milestone: Reverse the order of integration in a double integral. Apply Stokes' Theorem to compute a surface integral. Derive the gradient descent update rule for a simple quadratic loss function.
modules.ai_integration: Gradient descent, backpropagation chain rule, loss surface visualisation. Ask AI to animate gradient descent on a contour plot.

modules.name: Computation: Python for Mathematics
modules.description: Builds fluency in Python (NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib, SymPy) for numerical and symbolic mathematics. Not a software engineering course — focused entirely on mathematical applications.
modules.why_it_matters: Computation is the practical layer running alongside theory throughout the whole programme. You will use Python in every subsequent phase.
learning_outcomes: Set up a Python environment and use Jupyter notebooks for mathematical exploration.
Use NumPy for matrix operations, eigenvalue computation, and array broadcasting.
Use SciPy for integration, ODE solving, and optimisation.
Use SymPy for symbolic differentiation, integration, and algebra.
Visualise functions, vector fields, and surfaces with Matplotlib.
Write clean, mathematical code with appropriate documentation.
modules.milestone: Jupyter notebook that: (1) computes LU decomposition; (2) solves an ODE numerically; (3) plots a vector field with stream lines; (4) symbolically differentiates a multivariable function.
modules.ai_integration: Ask AI to generate boilerplate code for each new library. Ask AI to review your notebook for Pythonic improvements.

modules.name: Real Analysis
modules.description: The hardest and most important module in the programme. Formalises everything from Phase 1: what is a limit? What does continuous really mean? Why does the integral work? Be patient — most people struggle here before understanding it.
modules.why_it_matters: Real Analysis is the bedrock of all pure mathematics. Topology, Measure Theory, Functional Analysis, and Complex Analysis all require it. It is also the intellectual turning point of the programme.
learning_outcomes: Work with the axioms of the real number system, especially the Completeness Axiom.
Prove convergence and divergence of sequences using the ε-N definition.
Prove continuity of functions using the ε-δ definition; apply the Intermediate Value Theorem and Extreme Value Theorem.
Prove differentiability; apply the Mean Value Theorem rigorously.
Construct the Riemann integral via Darboux sums; prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Analyse uniform vs. pointwise convergence of sequences and series of functions.
Construct proofs about pathological functions (e.g., Dirichlet's function, Weierstrass function).
modules.milestone: Prove a bounded monotone sequence converges. Prove the composition of continuous functions is continuous. Prove the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous. Show there exists a continuous function that is nowhere differentiable.
modules.ai_integration: Proof checking and ε-δ argument critique. Ask for counterexamples to statements you think might be false. Ask AI to explain why each axiom of ℝ is necessary.

modules.name: Abstract Algebra
modules.description: Identifies the structural patterns that appear across all of mathematics. Groups abstract symmetry; rings abstract arithmetic; fields abstract number systems. Applications in cryptography, coding theory, and geometry.
modules.why_it_matters: Abstract Algebra reveals that the same patterns (groups, rings) appear in number theory, geometry, physics, and computer science. It trains the mind to work with structure rather than specific objects.
learning_outcomes: Define groups, subgroups, cosets, and normal subgroups; apply Lagrange's Theorem.
Classify finite abelian groups; apply the Sylow Theorems.
Understand and apply group homomorphisms and the Isomorphism Theorems.
Work with rings, ideals, and quotient rings.
Understand fields, field extensions, and basic Galois theory.
Apply abstract algebra to symmetry groups, modular arithmetic, and polynomial rings.
modules.milestone: Classify all groups of order ≤ 8. Find all subgroups of ℤ/12ℤ. Prove the First Isomorphism Theorem. Show ℤ[√−1] is a Euclidean domain.
modules.ai_integration: Counterexample generation: ask for concrete groups satisfying or failing specific properties. Ask AI to verify a claimed isomorphism.

modules.name: Ordinary Differential Equations
modules.description: The language of dynamics in physics, biology, economics, and engineering. Exact solution methods, qualitative analysis, phase plane techniques, and numerical methods.
modules.why_it_matters: ODEs model everything that changes over time. Systems of ODEs via matrix methods connect directly to linear algebra. Phase plane analysis is the geometric lens on dynamics — essential for stochastic calculus later.
learning_outcomes: Solve first-order ODEs (separable, linear, Bernoulli, exact).
Solve second-order linear ODEs with constant coefficients (homogeneous and inhomogeneous).
Use Laplace transforms to convert and solve initial value problems.
Solve systems of linear ODEs via matrix methods and matrix exponentials.
Construct phase portraits; classify critical points by stability.
Model real-world problems as ODEs and interpret solutions.
Apply Euler's method and Runge-Kutta numerically in Python.
modules.milestone: Solve a damped harmonic oscillator completely. Sketch the phase portrait for a nonlinear system and classify all critical points.
modules.ai_integration: Stability of gradient flows; neural ODEs; dynamical systems perspective on learning algorithms.

modules.name: Probability and Mathematical Statistics
modules.description: Probability is the mathematical framework for uncertainty. Goes well beyond NCEA — full calculus-based treatment of random variables, expectation, the CLT, and Bayesian inference.
modules.why_it_matters: The foundation for statistical learning, stochastic processes, and Bayesian AI. The Central Limit Theorem is the mathematical justification for most of classical statistics.
learning_outcomes: Work with probability spaces, events, and conditional probability; apply Bayes' Theorem.
Define and work with discrete and continuous random variables via PMFs, PDFs, and CDFs.
Calculate expectation, variance, covariance, and moment generating functions via integration.
State and apply the Central Limit Theorem and the Law of Large Numbers.
Perform maximum likelihood estimation.
Perform Bayesian inference and understand the frequentist vs. Bayesian distinction.
modules.milestone: Derive the distribution of the sum of two independent exponential random variables. Prove the CLT for iid variables via MGFs. Compute a Bayesian update for a Beta-Binomial model.
modules.ai_integration: MLE for neural networks; Bayesian deep learning; probabilistic graphical models. Ask AI to explain the connection between entropy and model uncertainty.

modules.name: Complex Analysis
modules.description: One of the most beautiful areas of mathematics. Complex differentiability is far more restrictive than real differentiability, making complex functions remarkably well-behaved. The Residue Theorem gives a powerful method for evaluating difficult real integrals.
modules.why_it_matters: Residues solve integrals that defeat real methods. Conformal mappings appear in fluid dynamics and electrostatics. Complex analysis is a prerequisite for Functional Analysis and parts of Stochastic Calculus.
learning_outcomes: Work with complex numbers in Cartesian, polar, and exponential form.
Define and verify analyticity via the Cauchy-Riemann equations.
Integrate complex functions along curves; apply Cauchy's Integral Theorem and Formula.
Classify and compute residues at isolated singularities.
Evaluate difficult real integrals using the Residue Theorem.
Understand conformal mappings and their geometric interpretation.
Represent functions as Laurent series and determine regions of convergence.
modules.milestone: Evaluate ∫_{-∞}^∞ 1/(1+x²)² dx using the Residue Theorem. Prove the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra using complex analysis. Show an analytic function on a disk is determined by its boundary values.
modules.ai_integration: Ask AI to generate Python code to visualise complex mappings (domain colouring). Ask for intuition behind why complex differentiability is so much stronger than real differentiability.

resources.title: A First Course in Complex Analysis
resources.author: Jiří Lebl

resources.title: Complex Analysis: An Open Source Textbook
resources.author: Russell Howell

modules.name: Advanced Linear Algebra
modules.description: Revisits linear algebra at a proof-based level. Jordan canonical form, spectral theorems, bilinear forms, and inner product spaces. The algebraic foundation for functional analysis and quantum mechanics.
modules.why_it_matters: The Spectral Theorem is one of the most important results in mathematics, underpinning PCA, quantum mechanics, and the theory of PDEs. Jordan form completes the classification of linear operators.
learning_outcomes: Classify linear operators via the Jordan canonical form.
Work with inner product spaces; prove the Gram-Schmidt theorem rigorously.
State and prove the Spectral Theorem for self-adjoint operators.
Classify bilinear and quadratic forms via signature.
Understand the connection between linear algebra over ℝ, ℂ, and general fields.
Apply results to least-squares, PCA, and operator theory.
modules.milestone: Prove the Cayley-Hamilton Theorem. Find the Jordan form of a given matrix. State and prove the Real Spectral Theorem. Explain geometrically why symmetric matrices always have orthogonal eigenvectors.
modules.ai_integration: Ask AI to connect each result to its ML application. Ask for clarification on how Jordan form relates to matrix exponentials for ODEs.

modules.name: Partial Differential Equations
modules.description: PDEs govern heat, waves, diffusion, fluid flow, and the Black-Scholes equation of financial mathematics. Fourier series and transforms are the central tools.
modules.why_it_matters: The heat equation governs diffusion processes — including generative AI (diffusion models are literally reverse-time stochastic heat equations). The Black-Scholes PDE underlies modern options pricing.
learning_outcomes: Classify second-order PDEs (elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic).
Apply separation of variables to the heat equation, wave equation, and Laplace equation.
Compute Fourier series and Fourier transforms; apply the convolution theorem.
Solve boundary value problems using Sturm-Liouville theory.
Understand the method of characteristics for first-order PDEs.
Apply Fourier transforms to solve the heat equation on ℝ.
Implement explicit finite difference schemes numerically in Python.
modules.milestone: Solve the heat equation on [0,L] with Dirichlet boundary conditions using Fourier series. Solve the wave equation via d'Alembert's formula. Implement a finite difference scheme for the 2D Laplace equation in Python.
modules.ai_integration: Diffusion models in generative AI. Ask AI to explain the connection between the heat equation and Brownian motion.

modules.name: Metric Spaces and Topology
modules.description: Metric spaces generalise distance to abstract settings; topology generalises metric spaces further. This is the language of Analysis, Functional Analysis, and Differential Geometry.
modules.why_it_matters: Required for Measure Theory and Functional Analysis (M4.1). Also the foundation for Topological Data Analysis — a growing area applying pure topology to data science.
learning_outcomes: Define metric spaces; verify the triangle inequality for examples (L¹, L², L∞, discrete).
Work with open sets, closed sets, and neighbourhoods in metric spaces.
Prove continuity via the open-set definition and compare to ε-δ definition.
Define and prove compactness; apply the Heine-Borel Theorem.
Define connected and path-connected spaces; give examples and non-examples.
Understand complete metric spaces and the Baire Category Theorem.
Define topological spaces as a generalisation and identify when a metric is topologically useful.
modules.milestone: Prove a closed subset of a compact metric space is compact. Prove the Banach Contraction Mapping Theorem. Show that C[0,1] with the sup norm is complete but not with the L¹ norm.
modules.ai_integration: Ask AI to generate examples of metric spaces that are not normed, or compact but not Euclidean. Use as a counterexample-generating tool.

resources.title: Basic Analysis I — Metric Space chapters
resources.author: Jiří Lebl

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.S190 Introduction to Metric Spaces
NTNU Topology Exercise Sheet 0 — Metric Spaces

modules.name: Numerical Analysis
modules.description: Bridges pure mathematics with computation. All analytical solutions are exact; all computations are approximate. Understanding when and why algorithms fail is critical for anyone using mathematics in practice.
modules.why_it_matters: Infinite-precision mathematics does not exist in a computer. The difference between a theoretically correct and a numerically stable algorithm is the difference between a model that works and one that crashes in production.
learning_outcomes: Analyse floating-point arithmetic and understand sources of numerical error.
Apply and compare iterative solvers for linear systems (Gauss-Seidel, conjugate gradient).
Implement polynomial interpolation (Lagrange, Newton) and splines.
Compute numerical derivatives and integrals with error bounds.
Apply Runge-Kutta methods for ODE initial value problems; analyse stability.
Understand the condition number of a matrix and its implications for linear system solving.
Implement QR decomposition via Householder reflections.
modules.milestone: Implement Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting. Implement RK4 and compare to Euler for a stiff ODE. Use SVD to compress an image to rank 10 and plot error vs. rank.
modules.ai_integration: Ask AI to explain why a specific scheme is unstable. Ask for a comparison of time complexity between iterative solvers.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.335 Introduction to Numerical Methods

modules.name: Measure Theory and Functional Analysis
modules.description: The rigorous foundation for probability theory, quantum mechanics, and PDE theory. Measure theory replaces informal 'area' with a precise framework. Functional Analysis studies infinite-dimensional vector spaces — the natural setting for PDEs and ML theory.
modules.why_it_matters: This is where self-study reaches true graduate level. Itô calculus (for stochastic finance) requires measure-theoretic probability. Hilbert spaces are the natural home of quantum mechanics, PDEs, and kernel methods in ML.
learning_outcomes: Define σ-algebras, measures, and Lebesgue integration; compare with the Riemann integral.
Prove the Monotone Convergence Theorem and Dominated Convergence Theorem.
Define L^p spaces and prove completeness (Riesz-Fischer Theorem).
Work with Banach spaces; prove the Hahn-Banach Theorem and Open Mapping Theorem.
Define Hilbert spaces; prove the Riesz Representation Theorem.
Understand spectral theory for bounded self-adjoint operators.
Connect measure theory to rigorous probability (probability spaces, conditional expectation).
modules.milestone: Construct the Lebesgue integral and prove the Dominated Convergence Theorem. Prove L²[0,1] is a Hilbert space. Prove the Riesz Representation Theorem.
modules.ai_integration: Ask AI to explain why the Lebesgue integral is more powerful than Riemann. Ask for the connection between Hilbert spaces and kernel methods in ML.

modules.name: Statistical Learning Theory
modules.description: The mathematical foundations of machine learning: empirical risk minimisation, bias-variance tradeoff, kernel methods, and PAC learning. The why behind the algorithms.
modules.why_it_matters: Understanding the theory means understanding the limits of ML — when it generalises, when it fails, and what mathematical guarantees can be given. Required for serious ML research.
learning_outcomes: Formalise supervised learning as empirical risk minimisation.
Derive and interpret the bias-variance decomposition.
Analyse regularisation (ridge, lasso) through a probabilistic (MAP) lens.
Understand kernel methods and the kernel trick geometrically and algebraically.
State and apply PAC learning bounds (VC dimension, Rademacher complexity).
Implement and analyse gradient descent variants.
Understand the mathematical structure of neural networks as function approximators.
modules.milestone: Derive the closed-form solution for ridge regression and show it as MAP estimation with a Gaussian prior. Prove the VC dimension of halfspaces in ℝ^d is d+1. Implement gradient descent with momentum and compare convergence to plain SGD.
modules.ai_integration: Primary AI module. Ask AI to explain any algorithm from first principles, to generate problems at specific difficulty levels, and to critique your proofs of generalisation bounds.

resources.title: Understanding Machine Learning: From Theory to Algorithms
resources.author: Shalev-Shwartz & Ben-David

modules.name: Stochastic Calculus and Mathematical Finance
modules.description: The mathematics of random processes evolving in time. Brownian motion, Itô's lemma (the stochastic chain rule), stochastic differential equations, and the Black-Scholes model.
modules.why_it_matters: The language of quantitative finance. Black-Scholes, interest rate models, risk-neutral pricing, and high-frequency trading all rest on this framework.
learning_outcomes: Define Brownian motion and prove its key properties (nowhere differentiable, quadratic variation = t).
Construct the Itô integral and derive Itô's formula (stochastic chain rule).
Solve basic stochastic differential equations (geometric Brownian motion, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck).
Derive the Black-Scholes PDE from a replication argument.
Understand risk-neutral pricing and martingale measures.
Apply Girsanov's theorem for change of measure.
Simulate stochastic processes numerically in Python (Monte Carlo option pricing).
modules.milestone: Derive the Black-Scholes formula from the PDE using a change of variables. Simulate geometric Brownian motion and price a European call by Monte Carlo. Prove discounted asset prices are martingales under the risk-neutral measure.
modules.ai_integration: Ask AI to explain Itô's lemma vs. ordinary chain rule with a side-by-side comparison. Use Socratic mode to test understanding of martingale theory.

modules.name: Machine Learning Theory
modules.description: Theoretical foundations of modern deep learning: universal approximation, optimisation landscape analysis, information theory, and the mathematics of attention mechanisms.
modules.why_it_matters: The gap between using ML tools and understanding what they compute. Required for serious research into AI systems.
learning_outcomes: State and prove (simplified) Universal Approximation Theorem for neural networks.
Analyse optimisation landscapes of neural networks (saddle points, overparameterisation).
Understand information-theoretic foundations (entropy, mutual information, data processing inequality).
Analyse attention mechanisms and transformers from a linear algebraic perspective.
Apply concentration inequalities (Markov, Chebyshev, Hoeffding) to generalisation bounds.
Understand the Neural Tangent Kernel and infinite-width networks.
modules.milestone: Prove a two-layer network with sigmoid activations can approximate any continuous function on [0,1]^d. Derive the gradient of cross-entropy loss for a softmax classifier. Implement transformer attention from scratch in Python.
modules.ai_integration: Use Socratic mode for deep learning theory concepts. Ask AI to find flaws in your proof attempts for approximation theorems.

resources.title: Understanding Machine Learning: From Theory to Algorithms
resources.author: Shalev-Shwartz & Ben-David

phase_1_exit: Compute and interpret the SVD of a 3×4 matrix.
Evaluate a double integral by changing to polar coordinates.
Prove that a power series converges uniformly on a closed sub-interval of its radius.
Write a Python notebook that solves Ax = b numerically and plots error vs. condition number.
phase_2_exit: Give an ε-δ proof that x² is continuous at x = √2.
State and prove the Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem.
Find all subgroups of S₄ and determine which are normal.
Solve a system of 3 coupled ODEs using matrix exponentials.
Derive the distribution of the maximum of n iid Uniform[0,1] random variables.
phase_3_exit: Evaluate ∫_{-∞}^∞ cos(x)/(1+x²) dx using the Residue Theorem.
Prove the Banach Contraction Mapping Theorem and give a concrete application.
Solve the heat equation on [0,π] with u(0,t) = u(π,t) = 0 and u(x,0) = sin(x).
Implement Gaussian quadrature in Python and compare to the exact result.
Prove that C[0,1] with the sup norm is a Banach space.
phase_4_exit: Construct the Lebesgue integral and prove the Dominated Convergence Theorem.
Derive the Black-Scholes formula starting from geometric Brownian motion (Track A).
Prove a generalisation bound using Rademacher complexity (Track B).
Implement a transformer attention layer from scratch and test on a toy task (Track B).
